## Turns conversations into structured, audit-ready intelligence

Enterprise AI that turns recorded conversations into structured, searchable data—supporting workflows from interviews, meetings, media, depositions and clinical notes.

### Structured Conversation Objects

Every conversation becomes a structured dataset — not a block of text. Including:

* Speakers (named, labeled, time-aligned)
* Topics & sections
* Key statements & decisions
* Action items
* Metadata (timestamps, confidence, language, source)

[Explore the technology](/features/automated-transcription)

### Conversation Modeling

Sonix automatically identifies speakers, tracks who said what, and preserves conversational context — even across interruptions, overlaps, and accents.

[Learn more](/features/automated-transcription)

### Structured Insights

Generate structured summaries designed for downstream use — not just reading.

[Get insights](/features/automated-summaries)

### Multi-Transcript Analysis

Ask questions across entire folders of transcripts to surface insights no single conversation reveals.

[Explore](/features/ai-analysis)

# The voice platform for every language

Speech-to-text, text-to-speech, and translation built for real-time products with unmatched accuracy in 60+ languages.

Trusted by teams building global voice products

## Multilingual voice AI for real-time applications

Power your products with speech-to-text, text-to-speech, and real-time translation in 60+ languages through one unified API.

[![](/images/logos/stt_full.svg)Speech-to-Text API

### Transcribe in real-time

Transcribe speech in real time across 60+ languages, with native-speaker accuracy for multilingual, language-switching, and multi-speaker conversations.

Explore Speech-to-Text API](/speech-to-text)[![](/images/logos/tts_full.svg)Text-to-Speech API

### Generate natural speech

Generate natural, high-fidelity speech in 60+ languages, with precise handling of alphanumerics, names, borrowed words, and language switching.

Explore Text-to-Speech API](/text-to-speech)[![](/images/logos/translation_full.svg)Speech Translation API

### Translate speech in real-time

Translate speech in real time across 3,600 language pairs, with low-latency output before sentences finish and high-quality multilingual results.

Explore Speech Translation API](/speech-translation)

## Built for the hardest parts of voice AI

Most voice platforms were built for English first. Soniox is built for high accuracy across 60+ languages, seamless language switching, alphanumerics, and low-latency interaction.

### Native-speaker accuracy

Unmatched recognition accuracy across languages, accents, numbers, names, and domain-specific vocabulary, engineered for fast, multi-speaker conversations and high-noise environments.

![Soniox Multilingual Speech-to-Text API](/content/img/2026/apr/layer-feature-sttaccuracy.webp)

### Text-to-speech built for precision

Generate high-fidelity, hallucination-free speech in 60+ languages. Built for the hardest production TTS challenges: alphanumerics, foreign names, language switching, and ultra-low-latency streaming.

![Soniox Multilingual Text-to-Speech API](/content/img/2026/apr/layer-feature-ttsaccuracy.webp)

### Low-latency streaming for live interaction

Transcribe speech with sub-200ms latency and start generating audio from the first few words, before the full sentence is available.

![Soniox Multilingual Speech-to-Text API](/content/img/2026/apr/layer-feature-streaming.webp)

### Translation for multilingual conversation

Real-time, context-aware translation across 60+ languages and 3,600 language pairs, engineered for code-switching environments where speakers switch languages mid-sentence.

![Soniox Multilingual Text-to-Speech API](/content/img/2026/apr/layer-feature-translation.webp)

![](/content/img/2026/apr/regions-map.webp)

## One global API, deployed locally

Use the same models and API everywhere, with in-region processing to meet latency, data residency, and regulatory requirements.

[Soniox Data Residency](/docs/data-residency)

## Built for agents, dictations, and everything in between

From real-time conversations to large-scale workflows, Soniox gives developers a complete speech platform for building fast, accurate, multilingual voice products.

### Voice agents

Power conversational AI with low-latency speech recognition and natural speech output built for responsive, human-like interactions.

![Soniox is used to build Voice Agents](/content/img/2026/apr/usecases-voiceagents.webp)

### Wearables

Deliver live voice experiences on devices that need streaming speech recognition and speech generation with minimal delay.

![Soniox is used to build Wearables](/content/img/2026/apr/usecases-wearables.webp)

### Speech translation

Translate spoken content in real time across 60+ languages with high accuracy. Build speech-to-text or speech-to-speech translation directly into your product.

![Soniox is used to build Wearables](/content/img/2026/apr/usecases-speechtranslation.webp)

### Dictation and voice typing

Turn speech into clean, reliable text for messages, notes, documents, and workflows where accuracy matters.

![Soniox is used to build Voice Agents](/content/img/2026/apr/usecases-dictation.webp)

Stop stitching together voice providers. One voice platform for speech-to-text, text-to-speech, and translation in 60+ languages. Built for low latency, multi-region deployment, and unmatched multilingual accuracy.

[Explore API](/platform)

## Frequently asked questions

What is Soniox?

Soniox is a real-time voice AI platform that turns speech into text and translations instantly. It works across 60+ languages and powers both the **Soniox App** for individuals and teams, and a **Speech-to-Text API** for developers and enterprises.

What does “speech AI” mean?

Speech AI or Voice AI refers to systems that understand spoken language in real time. Soniox goes beyond basic transcription by handling live speech, multiple speakers, mixed languages, punctuation, formatting, and real-world conversations as they happen.

What can I do with the Soniox App?

With the Soniox App, you can:

- Transcribe conversations live
- Translate speech in real time between languages
- Dictate text into any app or text field
- Capture meetings, notes, and ideas automatically

All on mobile and desktop, with one subscription.

What’s the difference between the Soniox App and the API?

**Soniox App** is a ready-to-use product for individuals and teams.

**Soniox API** is for developers who want to build speech recognition, translation, or voice-powered features into their own applications.

Both use the same underlying speech AI models.

Does Soniox offer a general-purpose speech-to-text API?

Yes. Soniox provides a production-ready, real-time speech-to-text and translation API designed for live applications, [voice agents](/speech-to-text/use-cases/voice-agents), meetings, and large-scale enterprise systems.

Can Soniox handle mixed languages in the same conversation?

Yes. Soniox can accurately recognize and transcribe conversations where speakers switch languages mid-sentence or mid-conversation — without needing manual language selection.

Can Soniox distinguish between different speakers?

Yes. Soniox supports speaker detection, allowing transcripts to clearly separate who said what, even in fast-paced or overlapping conversations.

Is Soniox suitable for developers and enterprise use?

Absolutely. Soniox is built for mission-critical use cases, offering:

- Low-latency real-time streaming
- High accuracy across accents and domains
- Scalable infrastructure
- Enterprise-grade security and compliance options

What makes Soniox different from other speech-to-text solutions?

Soniox is optimized for **real-world speech**, not just clean audio. It delivers:

- Native-speaker accuracy across 60+ languages
- Real-time transcription without waiting for sentence boundaries
- Mixed-language support
- Strong handling of numbers, names, and domain-specific terms
- A single platform powering both an app and an API

Do I need to be a developer to use Soniox?

No. If you want to transcribe, translate, or dictate speech, you can start immediately with the Soniox App. Developers can use the API to build custom voice-enabled applications.

How do I get started?

You can:

- **Get the App** to start using Soniox immediately, or
- **Build with API** to integrate Soniox into your product or workflow

Both options are available without long-term commitments.

## About this app

*arrow\_forward*

**Transkriptor: The Ultimate AI-Powered Speech-to-Text App and Productivity Tool**

Save time with Transkriptor, your all-in-one AI assistant for transcription and AI note-taking. Easily transcribe meetings, convert voice notes and memos to text, and transform videos into written notes. Transkriptor combines powerful AI features with convenience and speed for a seamless experience.

Key Features of Transkriptor:
**★ AI Assistant:** Transkriptor’s AI assistant enhances your workflow by generating AI summaries, drafting emails, and converting voice memos into text, helping you efficiently manage and interact with your transcripts.
 **★ AI Chat:** Interact directly with your transcripts using AI chat. Ask questions, extract important details, and get summaries—all through a user-friendly chat interface that saves time and boosts productivity.
 **★ AI Voice Memos:** Automatically transcribe voice memos with AI-powered precision. Capture every detail from your voice recordings, turning them into easy-to-read text that saves time and keeps your notes organized.
**★ Convert Voice Notes to Text:** Effortlessly convert voice notes to text with high accuracy, supporting various formats like MP3, WAV, and M4A. Ideal for transcribing voice memos, meetings, lectures, interviews, and more.
**★ Convert Video to Text:** Perfect for creating captions, transcribing video content, or generating notes from webinars and Youtube videos ensuring nothing is missed.
**★ Take Notes with Voice:**Use Transkriptor’s integrated audio recorder to capture lectures, personal thoughts, or any spoken content directly from your device. Easily take notes with voice, then navigate your recordings and transcripts with AI chat for quick access to key points.
 **★ Transcribe Voice Memos to Text:**  Convert voice memos into clear, organized notes with exceptional accuracy. Use our meeting assistant to capture every detail and create knowledge bases from transcriptions, ensuring all important information is documented and accessible.

Why Choose Transkriptor?
1. Save Time and Enhance Efficiency: Transkriptor’s AI features like the AI assistant, AI chat, and voice memo transcription reduce the time spent on manual note-taking and transcription. Let our AI handle the details while you focus on what matters most.
2. Versatility Across Formats: Whether transcribing voice notes, converting audio to text, or converting video to text, Transkriptor provides a comprehensive solution for all your transcription needs in one intuitive app.
3. Flexible and Affordable Plans: Start with our free plan, offering 90 minutes of transcription to explore our features, including AI chat and AI summary.
4. Secure and Private: Transkriptor ensures your data is securely stored and kept confidential, never shared with third parties.

Additional Features to Enhance Your Experience:
※ Import and Transcribe: Easily upload audio and video files for transcription. Transkriptor supports multiple formats, catering to a variety of use cases.
※ Export in Multiple Formats: Download your transcripts in formats like PDF, DOCX, TXT, and SRT, perfect for documentation, sharing, and archiving.
※ Speaker Identification: Automatically tag different speakers in your transcripts, making it easier to follow conversations with multiple voices.
※ Translation and Language Support: Transkriptor supports transcription in over 40 languages, making it perfect for global users and multilingual environments.
※ Organize with AI: Efficiently manage your transcripts with AI-powered folders, tags, and search features to quickly locate what you need.
Get Started with Transkriptor Today!

## With Wordly, nobody misses a word

### **How Much Does Wordly Cost?**

Wordly delivers 4 products for the cost of 1 translator. Wordly makes every meeting more accessible, engaging, and inclusive - across every language and format. Listen to translated audio; read translated subtitles; read same language captions; access text/voice transcripts and summaries. Wordly does it all for one affordable price.

[See All Live Translation Features](/live-translation)

### **What Event Formats and Use Cases Does Wordly Support?**

Wordly works great for in-person, virtual, and hybrid events - across a wide range of use cases. Whether you are training new employees; hosting an industry conference; running city council meetings; or dozens of other use cases, we have you covered.

‍[See All Use Cases](/translation-services)

### **What Attendee Access Options Does Wordly Offer?**

Attendees can access Wordly AI translation from their personal device - phone, tablet, or computer. And listen or read in their preferred language. Meeting organizers can also allow attendees to read captions and subtitles on monitors in the room.

[See How Translation Software Works](/translation-software)

### **What AI Translation Languages Does Wordly Support?**

Attendees and Speakers can each select their preferred languages - including the top international business languages and over 3,000 language pairs. Presenters can speak in dozens of languages (two-way translation), enabling you to support "one to many" and "many to many" sessions. Attendees can read a live transcript or listen to audio output.

‍[See All Translation Languages](/language-interpretation)

### **How Accurate are Wordly Translations and Captions?**

We do extensive language testing and ongoing AI translation engine optimization. Plus provide powerful glossary tools to ensure you always get high quality output. We also make the end to end experience easy to use for event organizers and attendees.

‍[Learn About Translation Quality](/wordly-interpretation-quality)

### **What Integrations Does Wordly Support?**

Wordly works with Cvent, Zoom, Teams, Encore, and many more event platforms and service providers. You can also connect directly to the mixer and display a live transcript on the stage or let attendees read / listen to translations on their mobile phone.

[See All Integration Options](/company/partners)

### **What Security and Privacy Standards Does Wordly Meet?**

In addition to powerful features, high scalability, and great support, Wordly has built strong security and privacy protection into our products. Wordly meets SOC 2 Type II compliance requirements, is ISO 27001 certified, meets GDPR privacy standards, and continuously tests our infrastructure.

‍[Read About Wordly Security](/wordly-security)

# Top 6 Voice Translator Apps Powered by AI in 2025

Updated:2026-04-135mins

Content

Content

AI Transcription & Summary

Saving time and effort with Notta, starting from today!

[Free trial](https://app.notta.ai/signup?language=en&from=official)

Language barriers often cause miscommunication in both personal and professional situations, but voice translator apps help bridge the gap.

While working in an international team, I’ve dealt with my fair share of confused clients and colleagues. Clear communication is probably the most important factor for keeping projects on track.

While language issues are challenging, inaccurate translation apps can make things even worse.

After countless mix-ups with various translation tools, I decided to take matters into my own hands. I tested dozens of apps to see which ones deliver on their promises, looking for features like accuracy, ease of use, and wide language support.

Here are my recommendations for the top 6 voice translator apps.

## Top picks for voice translator apps

1.[Notta](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/voice-translator-app#notta): Best for accurate voice transcriptions & translations

2.[Google Translate](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/voice-translator-app#google-translate): Best for translating websites

3.[Apple Translate](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/voice-translator-app#translate-ios): Best for basic translations

4.[iTranslate](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/voice-translator-app#itranslate): Best for offline translation

5.[AI Voice Translator Translate](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/voice-translator-app#ai-voice-translator-translate): Best for translating on iOS devices

6.[Microsoft Translator](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/voice-translator-app#microsoft-translator): Best for Microsoft Office users

| Voice translator app | Supported platforms | Standout feature | Translation languages |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Notta | Web, iOS, and Android | Real-time transcription and translation with high accuracy. | 42 |
| Google Translate | Web, iOS, and Android | Translate images, websites, documents, and words. | 100+ |
| Apple Translate | iOS and iPadOS | Work system-wide on iPhone and iPad. | 19 |
| iTranslate | Web, iOS, and Android | Voice-to-voice translation and offline translation. | 100+ |
| AI Voice Translator Translate | iOS and Android | Voice recognition and audio output from the text to the translated language. | 100+ |
| Microsoft Translator | Web, iOS, and Android | Real-time translation between multiple languages. | 100+ |

## How to choose a good voice translator app

When choosing a translator app, accuracy is the most important factor. However, convenience, flexibility, ease of use, and sharing options matter too. I selected apps with advanced features like real-time transcription, multi-language support, and speaker identification.

Here's what I expect from a good voice translator app:

* Accuracy: Some tools can handle basic phrases but struggle with accents, casual speech, or technical terminology.
* Ease of Use: The best voice translator tools have a simple user interface with accessible settings, so they’re easy to use for both beginners and tech experts.
* Storage: A quality audio translator offers plenty of storage for your data. I prefer advanced apps that automatically save all my translations to a single device or cloud storage.
* Integration and Sharing Options: If you work in a team setting, it helps to use an AI audio translator that offers strong integration and collaboration features. The ability to share meeting summaries and translations with colleagues makes a big difference.

List of the top voice translator apps

To find the top voice translator apps, I compiled a list with over 20 popular options. Then I downloaded each app and tested important features like accuracy and ease of use. Here are my best picks:

### #1 Notta: Best for accurate voice transcriptions & translations

[Notta](https://www.notta.ai/en) is the best for transcriptions and translations because it's accurate and fast. Imagine real-time translation and transcription of all of your meetings with ~99% accuracy. No more dealing with translators, no more spending hours editing transcripts. Notta creates clean results that you can use right away.

It’s a great all-in-one tool that integrates with most popular meeting platforms, like Zoom and Google Meet. As a result, it’s a perfect option for personal use and international teams.

Out of all the apps on the list, Notta performed the best in my test. I uploaded an hour-long audio file, and Notta translated it in just five minutes. The results also came impressively close to its 99% accuracy claim.

Notta also creates AI summaries in 58 different languages, including German, Spanish, Japanese, Portuguese, and Italian. I found that these summaries really save time because I can quickly review meeting highlights without reading through the whole transcript.

The platform also offers more unique AI features than any other tool I have used to translate audio. For example, it has speaker identification that I found really useful when translating group discussions.

Pros

* Notta supports 40+ translation and 58 transcription languages.
* The AI transcription tool has a high accuracy rate of up to 98.86%.
* It has very reasonable pricing plans.

Cons

* You need an active Internet connection to use the [Notta Mobile App](https://www.notta.ai/en/mobile) or Web App.
* The free plan does not offer transcript translation.

Pricing

* Free Plan
* Pro Plan: $8.17 per user per month
* Business Plan: $16.67 per user per month
* Enterprise Plan: Contact Sales

### #2 Google Translate: Best for translating entire websites

![Google Translate app](https://www.notta.ai/pictures/notta-blog-6aefb5a3-d97c-44dd-a210-cfe1a5d50103.jpg)[Google Translate](https://translate.google.us/) is a feature by Google that helps you translate real-time conversations on your browser. It is simple and allows you to instantly translate audio to text, copy it, and then use it to create emails, messages, or other communication material.

The best thing about Google Translate is its accessibility and ease of use, since you don’t have to download third-party apps. It’s a great option for quick, one-off translations, but it lacks some of the advanced features that other apps offer.

On top of that, it’s not as accurate as other voice translators on this list. One [study](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/362987687_THE_ANALYSIS_OF_GOOGLE_TRANSLATE_ACCURACY_IN_TRANSLATING_PROCEDURAL_AND_NARRATIVE_TEXT) found that its accuracy ranges from 79% for procedural texts to 86% for narrative ones, and my own testing showed similar results. It’s best for simple words and phrases, not advanced or complex content.

Pros

* Google Live Translate is free with no hidden costs involved.
* It can translate images, websites,[documents](https://www.notta.ai/en/blog/document-summarizer), and words.
* It has a simple user interface.

Cons

* Some translation languages are not available for offline use.
* It is not always helpful for accurate words and phrases.
* The offline feature is only available for Android and iPhone users.

Pricing

* Free

### #3 Apple Translate: Best for basic translations

![Translate app for iOS devices](https://www.notta.ai/pictures/notta-blog-ffcbd3cb-73f9-44cd-8e63-1a7610429d88.jpg)[Apple Translate](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/translate/id1514844618) is a good AI audio translator for iOS devices with a simple layout for basic translations. Apple Translate also has a built-in dictionary if you want to double-check the meaning of a word in your translated text. It’s a decent option for iPhone users looking for a quick and simple way to translate audio.

However, when I tested the app with a famous Spanish poem, I noticed it mistranslated some words. Some users also complain about the lack of an extensive language database. Apple Translate currently supports only 19 languages, including English, Spanish, French, German, and Chinese.

Pros

* You can download a language pack and translate offline.
* The voice translator is completely free to use.
* It has an easy-to-use interface.

Cons

* It does not support image-to-text translation.
* It is not helpful with complex words and technical terms.
* It supports very few translation languages.

Pricing

* Free

### #4 iTranslate: Best for offline translation

![Translate voice translation feature](https://www.notta.ai/pictures/notta-blog-dc4e2dd9-fa73-4416-a554-4bc53021289b.jpg)

[iTranslate](https://itranslate.com/features/voice-translations) is a great choice if you’re looking for an app that not only converts spoken language to text, but can also scan images. It works on both Android and iOS devices, has a clean interface, and can translate audio in over 100 languages.

I liked the image translation feature, especially when I had to scan menus, cards, signs, or other text while traveling. I also found it convenient to speak directly in the app instead of recording and uploading an audio file.

Pros

* There’s an offline translation mode, but it's only available for paid users.
* It supports voice-to-voice conversations in 100+ languages.
* You can take photos of menus, cards, signs, and more and translate them.

Cons

* The free version has unwanted ads.
* Advanced features like camera and offline translation are only available for paid users.
* It is not 100% accurate with certain phrases and sentences.

Pricing

* Free Plan
* In-app purchases

### #5 AI Voice Translator Translate: Best for translating on iOS devices

![AI Voice Translator Translate app](https://www.notta.ai/pictures/notta-blog-f51224f6-5a9b-4087-b6b3-a2fb50b3f918.jpg)[AI Voice Translator Translate](https://apps.apple.com/us/app/ai-voice-translator-translate/id1387396772) has an intuitive and user-friendly interface that lets you upload and translate audio in over 100 languages. Its audio output makes it a great choice for iOS users who want to polish their spoken language skills.

This AI voice translator lets you control the sound output settings and listen to the translated text in both male and female voices. For example, you can hear how a person would answer a question in another language, which is great if you want to practice your listening comprehension.

Pros

* AI Voice Translator for iPad supports two voice output settings: male and female.
* It supports more than 100 languages, including French, Italian, Portuguese, and Spanish.
* The tool is free to download on iOS devices.

Cons

* It's only available for iOS users.
* The translated text might include some incorrect words or phrases.
* It does not offer extra features, like transcription.

Pricing

* Free
* In-app purchases

### #6 Microsoft Translator: Best for Microsoft Office users

![Microsoft Translator voice translation app](https://www.notta.ai/pictures/notta-blog-69fa07fd-8770-4e58-b763-01a02880a470.jpg)The [Microsoft Translator](https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.microsoft.translator&hl=en&gl=US) app works great on both Android and iOS devices. It offers more than 100 translation languages, including German, Greek, English, and Chinese.

What I love about Microsoft Translator is its ability to translate text and other data even when there is no active internet connection. It offers language packs that you can download to translate audio offline. This makes it a great option for travel or in places with no reliable Wi-FI.

Pros

* There are several translation methods available, including text, voice, and image.
* It's completely free to use.
* It integrates well with Microsoft Office tools.

Cons

* The translated text is not always 100% accurate.
* Its offline translations are limited.
* Some users are unhappy with the new user interface.

Pricing

* Free

## Which translation app should I use?

If you're looking for a translation app that delivers quick results with high accuracy, you should use [Notta](https://app.notta.ai/signup?language=en&from=official). The app lets you upload existing audio and video files or use the tool to transcribe and translate content in real time.

It’s available as a web app, mobile app, and Chrome extension, and all of your files are synced across devices. The added flexibility lets you access all your files at any point during the day, whether you're at home or at work.

On top of that, Notta offers advanced AI-powered features that many other apps don't, like automatic summaries, meeting assistants, and speaker identification, which are especially useful when breaking language barriers.

## Translate your voice to text effortlessly

Each of the voice translator apps on this list comes with different features, accuracy levels, and limitations. These tools listen to the speaker, understand what’s being said, and provide instant translations that can be helpful in business meetings or live customer interactions. If you're looking for an all-in-one app that combines translation, transcription, and summarization into one, try out [Notta](https://app.notta.ai/signup?language=en&from=official) today.

![Notta's real-time translation feature](https://www.notta.ai/pictures/notta-blog-d84bf633-c450-4466-bafb-886569e205ad.jpg)

[Try Notta for Free](https://app.notta.ai/signup?language=en&from=official)

## FAQs

### How do I translate live audio?

To translate live audio, you need software that supports real-time speech recognition and speech-to-text translation. Notta is the best app I’ve tested with this feature. It allows you to transcribe audio in real-time and then translate it into 40+ languages, including Japanese, Chinese, Arabic, and French.

Here's how to translate live audio via Notta:

* Open the Notta Web App on Windows/Mac or the Notta Mobile App on Android/iOS, and then select ‘Record an Audio.’
* Hold the device close to the audio source and watch Notta transcribe the speech to text in real-time.
* Once it's transcribed, click the ‘Translate’ option and then choose the target language to convert the transcript.

### What is the best app for voice language translation?

Notta is the best app for voice language translation for both business and personal use. It offers built-in transcription and translation features for recorded audio and video files, as well as real-time conversations. The multi-language support, high accuracy, and ease of use make Notta the best voice translator on the market.

# The Best Transcription Services

Updated September 22, 2025

![](https://cdn.thewirecutter.com/wp-content/media/2025/09/transcriptionservices-2048px-05682-2x1-1.jpg?width=150&quality=20&crop=2:1&auto=webp)![Someone using GoTranscript on a laptop.]()

Marki Williams/NYT Wirecutter

[![Matthew Guay](https://cdn.thewirecutter.com/wp-content/media/2024/09/matthew-guay.jpg?auto=webp&quality=60&crop=1:1&width=50)](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/authors/matthew-guay/)

By [Matthew Guay](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/authors/matthew-guay/)

FYI

After a new round of tests, we found that [GoTranscript](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/28878/148628/4/195016/?merchant=GoTranscript) is the best transcription service, with highly accurate human transcription aided by AI. [Fellow](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74293/225078/4/229203/?merchant=Fellow%20AI) is best for transcribing meetings into notes.

September 2025

Transcription is now a commodity, a default feature in the work-centered apps so many people use on a daily basis. Zoom, Google Meet, and Microsoft Teams transcribe meetings. Slack adds transcripts to audio shared in chat. Even YouTube auto-captions videos.

And the transcriptions are good, surprisingly so. In 2018, Wirecutter found the best AI transcription tools to be 73% accurate. Today, even the least accurate AI transcription is 94% accurate — and, surprisingly, the best AI transcription services, including OpenAI’s free Whisper transcription engine, are somewhat more accurate than the least-precise human-powered transcriptions.

After spending 35 hours testing 15 transcription tools, including human- and AI-powered options, we found that [GoTranscript](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/28878/148628/4/195016/?merchant=GoTranscript) is the best service for people who need the most accurate transcripts. If you’re willing to work with slightly less accurate but far faster AI transcription, [Vook.ai](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64660/206120/4/195033/?merchant=Vook.ai) is the best affordable transcription service.

For meetings, raw accuracy is less important than clear notes and tools that resurface important takeaways. ## The research

## Why you should trust us

I’ve been testing and writing about software professionally since 2010, and I have used both human- and AI-powered transcription services for interviews and meetings throughout my career. I extensively use dictation for mobile typing, across multiple languages, and I regularly use transcripts to build note summaries after meetings.

For this guide:

* I spent 35 hours researching 38 transcription services. I then tested 15 transcription services, including [separately testing](#how-we-picked-and-tested) human and AI transcription on three services and testing multiple transcription models on OpenAI Whisper.
* I spent an additional 10 hours in multiple live Zoom meetings testing eight tools built specifically for meeting transcription.
* I dug deep on articles, blogs, and forums related to transcription services, including recommendations from [NYU Libraries](https://guides.nyu.edu/QDA/transcription), [Harvard University](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/transcripts), and the [University of Michigan Library](https://guides.lib.umich.edu/c.php?g=839924&p=6982326).
* Wirecutter writer Kaitlyn Wells researched and wrote the original version of this guide, which was first published in 2018 and then updated in 2020. Discussion of some of her original testing remains in this version.
* Like all Wirecutter journalists, I review and test products with complete editorial independence. I’m never made aware of any [business implications](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/reviews/how-wirecutter-makes-money/) of my editorial recommendations. Read more about our [editorial standards](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/editorial-standards/).

## Who this is for

Anyone who records large amounts of spoken audio and video — for interviews, podcasts, videos, meetings, dictated notes, and more — can benefit from a transcription service.

Transcripts make finding specific quotes in recordings as easy as searching any other document. They increase note-taking speed and accuracy for journalists, legal and medical professionals, and others who rely on interviews and spoken conversations. And they make podcasts and videos accessible to a wider audience. As a side benefit, they help listeners search through shows and even aid search engines in indexing content.

These days, however, automatic transcripts for meetings in apps such as Slack and Microsoft Teams may be enough for you to get general notes, especially for quick, one-off calls or larger presentation meetings with little crosstalk. Automated YouTube transcripts could suffice when you’re starting a new channel. If transcripts are only a nice-to-have for your work, a free automated tool is the better option for you.

But if accurate transcripts are crucial enough to your work that you would otherwise spend hours painstakingly transcribing speech yourself or editing automated transcripts, paying for human transcription or higher-quality automated transcripts is the better choice. These services include features to share transcripts, edit them manually, and export the text in caption and subtitle formats. Meeting-transcription tools pair automated, time-stamped transcripts with AI-generated summaries and agenda items. All of these features are worth paying for when transcripts are a core part of your work, especially for meetings with more crosstalk and active participation, which make it difficult to keep up when you’re taking notes.

## What you should know about AI versus human transcription

Transcribing one minute of audio by hand takes, on average, four minutes, [according to Rev](https://www.rev.com/blog/resources/how-long-does-it-take-to-transcribe-audio-video) and other sources. That makes human-transcribed audio expensive, at a typical price of $1 to $3 per minute of audio.

Human transcription — powered by experience and intuition — gives you the highest-quality results, while slightly less-accurate AI transcription tools use neural-network transformer models that recognize spoken words in audio and then infer the correct written words and punctuation.

That guesswork leads to surprising mistakes. For example, in our testing “as large as” was transcribed by multiple AI services as “F/f.” Even more strangely, one service transcribed the decimal expression “1.82%” as “1.8199999999999998%.” Overall, today’s AI transcriptions are 96% accurate on average — close, but still noticeably incorrect at times.

Yet AI has raised the bar for transcription, a notion borne out by the fact that even human transcription is, on average, more accurate today than it was a half-decade ago. Today’s human-transcription services often use AI for a first pass before humans clean up the copy, and the most accurate transcripts in our testing came from these tag-team efforts.

## How we picked and tested

For this guide, we first considered how the field has changed since we first tested transcription services in 2018. Then we gathered recommendations from New York Times reporters, along with roundups from [The Verge](https://www.theverge.com/23316220/transcription-ai-otter-temi-how-to) and [PCMag](https://www.pcmag.com/picks/the-best-transcription-services). We also took into account recommendations from [NYU Libraries](https://guides.nyu.edu/QDA/transcription), the [University of Michigan Library](https://guides.lib.umich.edu/c.php?g=839924&p=6982326), and [Harvard University](https://accessibility.huit.harvard.edu/transcripts), among others, as well as forums and social media.

That research helped us home in on the features that are crucial to a transcription service, including:

* **Transcription accuracy:** Nothing matters more, and this is the key differentiating factor between paid services and the transcription tools built into free software.
* **Speed:** AI transcription should take no more than a few minutes to transcribe one minute of audio. Human transcription always takes longer, but the faster, the better.
* **Speaker labeling:** Transcriptions should split the text by speaker, with consistent labeling of each speaker and options to easily edit names.
* **Availability:** Modern transcription services should work on any phone or computer. Most transcription services today are web applications, accessible from any web browser, while some additionally include iOS and Android apps.
* **Supported file formats:** It should be easy to record audio in any application and then upload it to a transcription service. Services should support popular audio formats, including MP3, M4A, and WAV. They should also allow you to export transcripts in text format, as a Word document, and as a PDF, and ideally in subtitle and captioning formats such as SRT.
* **Editing features:** At a minimum, transcription services should make it easy for you to copy and export your completed transcript. Ideally, they should also include tools for you to listen to your audio and compare it against the transcript, with editing tools for you to tweak the text as needed. Additional Google Docs–style collaboration tools are a bonus.
* **Privacy focus:** Audio recordings often include personal details from meetings, client interactions, and more. Transcription services need to be designed around security, with policies in place to control who has access to your audio and when your audio is deleted, ideally paired with HIPAA compliance and options for signed NDAs.

With more than a dozen services on our must-test list, we then put four audio clips through each of them:

* a 443-word clip of 3 minutes 7 seconds, from an [audio version of a New York Times article about the United States Olympics shooting team](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/07/23/world/olympics/paris-olympics-shooting-travel.html), with studio-quality audio from a single speaker
* a 745-word clip of 4 minutes 19 seconds, from a [New York Times *The Interview* podcast about the Olympics](https://www.nytimes.com/2024/07/20/magazine/joel-embiid-interview.html), with clear audio of two speakers with different accents, including location names and crosstalk
* a 235-word clip of 2 minutes 21 seconds, from a video about speech issues and teaching in casual English with call-level audio quality from two speakers, including crosstalk and speech impediments
* a 333-word clip of 2 minutes 41 seconds, recorded for testing, captured in a noisy environment with a script including a pangram, names, scientific and technical terms, percentages, and numbers with decimals, with multiple speakers and accents

We submitted these clips to each service, testing both AI- and human-transcription options where possible. With human-transcription services, we added technical terms from the fourth script to custom dictionaries and paid for one-day turnaround time.

We compared the services’ interfaces and core features, including their supported audio formats and collaboration options. We also timed how long each service took to provide transcripts and respond to support queries, and we noted the total cost per minute.

With the transcripts in hand, we then compared each transcript against our original to assess readability and accuracy.

We noted where transcription services cleaned up text, removing filler words, but we didn’t count it against them. When the wrong word was used, though, we counted that against the service’s accuracy score.

For meeting-transcription services, we focused on the readability of the transcript, the accuracy of any automated meeting summaries, and the quality of the tools available to search through transcripts and highlight key takeaways.

Its editor is basic, but the service includes a number of features that can speed up your work. You can get a free AI transcript preview of the first two minutes of your audio, which allows you to determine whether the quality is sufficient before paying — and if it isn’t, you can upgrade to a human transcription. You get your transcript emailed as a Word document. And it’s HIPAA-compliant, so it’s ready for health-care use.

**Its human transcriptions were over 99% accurate in our tests.** The words that GoTranscript’s human transcribers missed were more likely to be filler words (for example, writing “while” instead of “and while”) rather than entirely incorrect words. Spoken names that other transcription services frequently misspelled (including “Sagen Maddalena,” “Will Shaner,” and “Joel Embiid”) were transcribed accurately. GoTranscript’s results were close enough to perfect that we had to look carefully for differences.

GoTranscript cleans up transcriptions for readability. Generally that’s good, though in our experience it sometimes affected quality. Filler words including “you know,” “like,” and “kind of” were removed, and a misspoken “there’s a qualified” was turned into the more accurate “there’s a qualifier.” And you have to watch out for apparent fillers that are actually modifiers, as in an instance where “kind of” was removed, which mildly altered the meaning of a sentence.

Of the other inaccuracies we found, “vlog” was mistaken as “blog,” “C#” was written as “CShark,” and “AI” came out as “AGI.” A single word — “Caucasus” — was marked as “[unintelligible]” rather than being guessed, which was what many other services would do.

To improve accuracy, you can email the GoTranscript team to add industry-specific flags to your account for medical, technical, and legal terms, or to add custom words to your account’s dictionary. But it lacks an in-app dictionary feature, something offered by competing services such as Rev and Scribie.

**Its AI transcripts were worse at names but still 98.9% accurate.** That’s a better result than what we got from all but one of the human-powered transcription services we tested, at a fraction of the time and price.

It missed one name (transcribing “Sagen” as “Sajan”) and used the British English spelling “catalogue,” with no option for us to choose between US and British English spelling. It also had a few issues with our final test clip: It wrote the pangram without spaces, rendered “AI” as “AVI,” and turned “streaming” into “steaming.” One clip had a phantom “Thank you” added to the end.

But the transcripts it made in our tests were clean and readable, and the AI handled crosstalk, repeated words, and other verbal issues well. The results are close enough to perfect that, for clear audio without uncommon words, you might be able to use them without any edits.

**The interface is designed for you to upload audio and get it transcribed as quickly as possible.** You can order transcription in around five clicks and download a completed transcript in a single click. When ordering, you can choose AI or human transcription, after which you can upload an audio file in nearly any popular format (MP3, M4A, WEBM, OGG, and video files including MP4 and WMV) or paste a link to a Dropbox or Google Drive file or a YouTube or Vimeo video.

You can listen to your audio synced with the text, as well, though you may need to reupload your audio if it has been several days since you ordered your transcript.

Collaboration is similarly basic: You can copy a sharing link to the transcription editor, where colleagues can edit the text but not actually save the edits. **It’s safe to use with sensitive information.** The service uses in-house transcription software and promises that only employees and transcribers who need to perform specific tasks can access personally identifiable info. ### Flaws but not dealbreakers

**You can’t place orders or view completed transcripts through the mobile app.** Although GoTranscript offers an iOS app, it can only upload audio to GoTranscript. Afterward, you need to go to the website to place your order and then read your completed transcript in the web app or download the transcript from your notification email.

**GoTranscript’s online editor is barebones.** You’ll find no formatting options or keyboard shortcuts. If you pay for time stamps in your transcripts, you can’t turn them off in the interface — the only way to remove them from the text is to edit them out manually. And the editor doesn’t include integrations with Dropbox, Zapier, or other popular automation tools.

**The cheapest rates require large orders and long turnaround times.** Human transcription starts at an advertised $1.02 per minute — but you can get that rate only by ordering over 10,000 minutes of transcription with a five-day turnaround. Three-day turnaround is listed as the most popular option, but that might seem prohibitively long for most needs today, when AI can offer a good-enough transcription in minutes.

You can toggle time stamps and speaker names, copy text, or export transcripts in Word, PDF, Markdown, or ST subtitle format.

**Its transcripts are 98.7% accurate on average.** In our tests, Vook.ai ranked second only to GoTranscript in AI-transcription accuracy. The results were good enough to use with only light edits.

A couple of names were misspelled, slightly more than with GoTranscript: “Roe” came back as “Rowe,” while “Bhupinder” wound up as “Bupinda.” It didn’t handle crosstalk as well as our top pick did and missed a few smaller words, including “did,” “they,” and “yeah.” And “percent” was written out as a word instead of with the percent symbol.

**It keeps transcripts in-house.** User data is stored on EU-based servers and RSA-encrypted. Transcriptions run on an OpenAI Whisper model run on Vook.ai’s infrastructure. “Audio files or transcripts are not shared to any third party,” the Vook.ai team told us, unless users request DeepL translation or GPT-powered transcript summaries.

Automated transcriptions don’t require a human to listen to audio or read the transcript, which keeps transcription private by design. Vook.ai’s service is not HIPAA-certified, though; as a result, GoTranscript is a better choice for medical and legal uses if compliance is required.

### Flaws but not dealbreakers

**It has no sharing or collaboration features.** Unlike our top pick, Vook.ai has no sharing or collaboration features, though it does have an email-export option to send a transcript directly to a colleague. Similar to GoTranscript, Vook.ai doesn’t offer universal search across all of your transcripts (something that Alice, Descript, and Rev provide). The only way to organize transcripts is by their read or unread status, or with tags.

## Best for transcribing and summarizing meetings: Fellow

![](https://cdn.thewirecutter.com/wp-content/media/2025/09/BEST-TRANSCRIPTION-SERVICES-03298.jpg?width=150&quality=20&crop=2048:1365&auto=webp)![Someone using Fellow on a laptop.]()

Marki Williams/NYT Wirecutter

#### Best for...

[![](https://d34mvw1if3ud0g.cloudfront.net/74293/Fellow_20250918-152722_full.jpeg)](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74293/225078/4/229163?merchant=Fellow%20AI)

## [Best for transcribing and summarizing meetings](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74293/225078/4/229163?merchant=Fellow%20AI "Best for transcribing and summarizing meetings")

### [Fellow](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74293/225078/4/229163?merchant=Fellow%20AI "Fellow")

This service offers the most well-rounded set of AI meeting-assistant features, with accurate transcripts and easy-to-scan meeting summaries that highlight key points.

[Buy from Fellow AI](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74293/225078/4/229163?merchant=Fellow%20AI)

Meeting-transcription software can keep you focused instead of scrambling to take accurate notes. [Fellow](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74293/225078/4/229203/?merchant=Fellow%20AI) stood out in our test of meeting-transcription apps for its genuinely useful summaries. It accurately transcribed meetings into readable dialogue and outlined the discussion into topics, and its AI chat accurately answered questions about what was said.

Most importantly, it highlighted key points in the outline and summary in bold to help us quickly skim through call summaries. That, along with its deep feature set, made Fellow more useful for reviewing calls than other meeting-transcription tools we tested.

**It can record audio and video from the meeting software you already use.** Compatible with Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and Google Meet, Fellow saves a complete video of every recorded call, showing the active speaker while they’re talking. Its video player is reminiscent of YouTube’s interface and highlights the corresponding part of the transcript while words are being spoken.

With speaker names and time stamps in both the transcript and the AI summary, we found it easy to jump back in and get more context from the discussion. It was also easy for us to skim through the video, with each section of the meeting from the AI-generated outline highlighted in the video’s progress bar.

![](https://cdn.thewirecutter.com/wp-content/media/2025/09/BEST-TRANSCRIPTION-SERVICES-Fellow.jpg?width=150&quality=20&crop=2048:1173&auto=webp)![A screengrab of Fellow AI transcription service.]()

Fellow

**It helps you prepare before meetings.** Whereas most call-recording apps show past recordings by default, Fellow is built around a calendar, with your upcoming Google or Outlook calendar meetings listed on a schedule. Each event entry includes space for you to add talking points, action items, and free-form notes before the call.

With a basic plan starting at $11 per month, Fellow can record 10 meetings per month with AI summaries. With a Business plan starting at $23 per month per user, Fellow can also recap previous recurring meetings to start out the new meeting, reminding you where the discussion left off last time.

During the meeting, Fellow shows precall notes in a sidebar. You can follow the agenda for the meeting, check off talking points as they’re discussed, and take notes or list new ideas as they come to mind during the call. You can’t, however, highlight specific moments during a meeting, as you can in Fathom or Otter.

**AI summaries are actionable and easy to scan.** Included is a one-paragraph summary of the call, along with a detailed topics outline that breaks the call down into broad sections. Key points in bold text allow you to easily skim through. You can edit the summary if you’d like, or you can have have Fellow’s AI regenerate the summary if it doesn’t seem accurate.

The topics outline can’t be edited, but Fellow’s X-Ray feature gives you a more detailed overview of a specific point with direct quotes from the transcript and time stamps for you to jump to each bit of the discussion. You can also use the built-in AI chatbot to summarize key takeaways, list questions that remained unanswered, and more. It was one of the most accurate AI chats we tested in identifying key ideas and ignoring less relevant details.

**Calls can be clipped and shared.** This is a feature missing from other meeting apps. Fellow also lets you share your transcript, meeting recordings, and AI summaries via Slack or email, complete with access to Fellow’s AI chat.

**It’s built with privacy in mind.** Fellow is HIPAA-compliant and SOC2-audited, and it includes options to opt out of voice detection. It also allows teams to create policies concerning which calls can be automatically recorded. It automatically announces when calls are being recorded, and it can email call participants to inform them ahead of time. Personal notes remain private, while only AI-generated summaries, transcripts, and topic recaps are shared with call participants.

### Flaws but not dealbreakers

**It doesn’t show live captions during the call.** Unlike some other meeting-transcription tools, including Otter and Granola, Fellow waits until after the call to both transcribe the audio and turn it into a meeting summary. Zoom and Google Meet both offer built-in live captions, though, and you can ask Fellow’s AI to summarize what has been said so far during a meeting.

**It doesn’t automatically detect new calls.** Fellow is built around scheduled meetings and can automatically join calls with Google Meet, Microsoft Teams, or Zoom links in your calendar; you can also manually add it to Zoom calls. But if you start a call on your computer, Fellow’s app doesn’t automatically open and ask if you’d like to start recording the call, as other meeting apps typically do.

**Auto-generated action items are inconsistent.** In our tests, while Fellow’s AI chat was very good at finding important details in calls, and its outline and meeting summary did a great job of listing out meeting discussion points, it didn’t always recognize tasks and turn them into to-dos.

## Other transcription services worth considering

![](https://cdn.thewirecutter.com/wp-content/media/2025/09/transcriptionservices-2048px-05662.jpg?width=150&quality=20&crop=2048:1365&auto=webp)![Someone on a laptop.]()

Marki Williams/NYT Wirecutter

**If you need to work on transcriptions as a team:** [Descript](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64661/206121/4/195018/?merchant=Descript) (free for one hour of transcripts per month; from $16 per month for 10 hours of transcripts) was designed as a tool for editing podcasts and videos, but it’s also a great AI-powered transcription service with a modern, collaborative editor reminiscent of [collaborative notes and the to-do list app Notion](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/reviews/best-to-do-list-app/).

It was 98.4% accurate in our tests, but it didn’t handle speech impediments or crosstalk well, especially in comparison with our top pick. It also failed to clean up filler speech or identify speakers by default, though it does include AI features to add speakers after the core transcription is complete.

Descript is worth considering for its interface alone if you’re working with a team. And if you’re building transcripts for a podcast, it could be an all-in-one tool for recording, editing, and transcribing podcasts as a team.

**If you want AI to enhance your hand-typed notes:** [Granola](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74177/224885/4/229204/?merchant=Granola) (free for 25 meetings; $18 per month for unlimited meetings) starts each call with a blank note where you can write rich text or Markdown notes while a live transcript runs underneath. When you finish the call, Granola’s AI expands your notes into a detailed summary, with AI chat for you to go back and ask questions about the meeting. It leaves your text in bold and adds context and details from the call in a lighter gray font for visual separation.

Uniquely, it lets you restart recording to pick up the discussion later but keeps all of the notes in one place. And if you write down anything that isn’t mentioned aloud during the call, Granola leaves those details in your personal, private meeting notes for your future reference, sharing just the AI-enhanced minutes and transcript with your team.

Granola is more basic than other meeting-recording apps. It doesn’t record audio or video, it separates dialogue into only a chat-style “you” and “others,” and it doesn’t let you edit speakers’ names. Instead, it allows you to direct the outcome of the AI-generated meeting summary more than any other app, which can fit better into your workflow if you’re already accustomed to writing detailed notes during a call.

With a minimal, streamlined interface that lets you collaborate with AI on your meeting notes, it reminded us of [Sunsama](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/reviews/best-ai-scheduling-apps/), another tool that takes a more human-guided approach to AI (with your schedule, in Sunsama’s case).

**If you want free, locally processed AI transcripts:** [OpenAI Whisper](https://github.com/openai/whisper) — the engine behind [Vook.ai](#best-for-fast-ai-transcriptions-vook-ai) and likely other commercial transcription tools — is an open-source, MIT-licensed, Python-powered transcription model that you can run for free on any computer. Its Large model was 98.7% accurate in our tests, while its faster Small model was 97.7% accurate.

It’s geeky: You can run it in Terminal on Macs and Linux PCs or in Command Prompt on Windows. It runs slowly on traditional Intel and AMD processors, but it can deliver transcripts as quickly as in one minute per minute of audio on modern ARM CPUs, including Apple’s M1 and newer, or on CUDA-compatible Nvidia GPUs.

Alternatively, you can use it in third-party wrappers, including [MacWhisper](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64662/206122/4/195022/?merchant=GoodSnooze) for Mac, [Aiko](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64663/206123/4/195023/?merchant=Apple%20App%20Store) for iOS, and [Speech Translate](https://github.com/Dadangdut33/Speech-Translate/) for Windows PCs with CUDA-compatible Nvidia GPUs. Or, you could use hosted versions of Whisper via automation tools like [Zapier](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/40562/225079/4/229205/?merchant=Zapier) or [Make](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74212/224949/4/229206/?merchant=Make), or build it into in-house apps with [OpenAI’s Whisper API](https://openai.com/index/whisper/).

It doesn’t include speaker tracking or time stamps, which may make it less attractive for some uses. But it’s the transcription model to beat today, and we’d be remiss not to mention it.

## The competition

*This is not a comprehensive list of all transcription services we have tested. We have removed any services that are no longer available or do not meet our current requirements.*

[Transcription Panda](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64664/206124/4/195025/?merchant=TranscriptionPanda), a human-powered transcription service, delivered the most accurate transcription we encountered in our tests — but it costs nearly $1 more per minute than GoTranscript with one-day turnaround and takes nearly six times as long to transcribe the same audio. It’s also notably basic, with no sharing, editing, or playback features. The few errors we saw changed the meaning of sentences more than those from GoTranscript’s human transcribers.

[Happy Scribe](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64665/206125/4/195026/?merchant=Happy%20Scribe) offers both human and AI transcription, and the accuracy of its human transcription came in just under GoTranscript’s AI accuracy. It handled crosstalk well, and text was highly readable, but it was often altered from the spoken record. “Did they set a timeline?” became “What was the timeline that they set?” That’s somewhat close, but the meaning was changed. While its editor is more modern than our top pick’s and includes extra collaboration features, it doesn’t allow multiple people to edit a transcript at the same time.

[Scribie](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/28883/148633/4/195027/?merchant=Scribie) is the most affordable human-powered transcription service we tested, at just 80 cents per minute. First it uses AI to transcribe your audio, and then human editors clean up the copy. Scribie was as accurate as Happy Scribe, though with a few more spelling differences than other transcription services. It offers Word, PDF, and text-format export, but no subtitle or caption formats. It was very good at handling crosstalk and repetitive or filler words, though.

[Rev](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/28882/148632/4/195028/?merchant=Rev) offers both human- and AI-powered transcription and has the most modern, professional editor we’ve seen. Unfortunately, it wasn’t as accurate as our current picks, with 98% accurate human transcription and only 96.7% accuracy with AI. Names were especially troublesome for AI, and while the human transcriptions were better, they included some AI-like artifacts, including “as large as” being written as “f / f.”

[Reduct](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64666/206126/4/195029/?merchant=Reduct) is a video-centric take on what Descript does for podcast editing. Transcripts show up in real time as the AI completes them, with a quick first pass (taking one minute per minute of audio) followed minutes later by a more accurate final transcription (around three minutes per minute of audio, which is slow for AI transcription). In our tests it missed an entire phrase on the end of one clip and added extraneous phrases to the end of two other clips. However, it could be worth considering if you’re primarily transcribing captions for videos, as long as you edit the text before publication.

[Alice](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/64667/206127/4/195030/?merchant=Alice) is a basic AI transcription service built around automation. Finished transcripts are emailed to you and any colleagues you add, or Alice can save them to Dropbox, Google Drive, or OneDrive, share them on Slack, add them to Trello tasks, or kick off Zapier automations. In our tests, it whiffed on a number of names and words, transcribing “Cameroon” as “Kenmore,” “bobcats” as “bug cats,” and “bag” as “Baghdad,” among other mistakes that we didn’t see in other transcripts. But its built-in search was better than that of competitors, surfacing results across every transcript in our account.

[Trint](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/28881/148631/4/195031/?merchant=Trint) is designed around transcribing large volumes of audio every month. It offers unlimited AI transcription starting at $60 per month in a collaborative editor complete with full-text search, sharing, and comments. It includes a full-featured mobile app so that you can get text transcribed on the go, and it supports exports in a wide variety of subtitle formats. Unfortunately, it was just 95% accurate in our tests and made errors that changed meaning, such as transcribing “steady” as “study,” “nebulously” as “nebulous Lee” and “That’s always gonna” as “I was always going to.”

[Otter](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/28880/148630/4/229207/?merchant=Otter) is the sole service we tested both for transcription and for meeting briefings, as it includes audio recording, transcription, and meeting-summary tools in one app. It’s a polished app with a cleaner interface than many other meeting tools offer. Its accuracy was lower than that of other transcription-focused software, though, and its meeting transcripts didn’t separate speakers or filter out disfluencies as effectively as other meeting-transcription software.

[Fathom](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74214/224951/4/229208/?merchant=Fathom) is a basic meeting-transcription app focused on meeting summaries. It records full videos of meetings, shows live captions during calls, summarizes meetings with key takeaways and topic outlines, and includes a highlight feature for you to manually note important discussions during a meeting. Unfortunately, its interface was the most basic of the apps we tested, and its search takes longer than others to index transcripts.

[Fireflies](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74223/224960/4/229209/?merchant=Fireflies.ai) supports 100 languages — the most of any service we tested — and its meeting overview and transcripts were easy to follow, with time stamps to jump to the relevant bit of recorded audio. Its AI chat wasn’t good at finding answers from the meeting transcript, though, and you have to manually request meeting summaries.

[Tactiq](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74224/224961/4/229210/?merchant=Tactiq) records with a Chrome add-on, requiring you to take your calls in your browser. That’s more of a hassle than with other services, which can use Zoom bots or native Mac and Windows apps. (It has a native Mac app in beta.) It also requires you to manually request a meeting summary, and it doesn’t record audio or video natively.

[Krisp](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/74225/224962/4/229211/?merchant=Krisp)’s original purpose was to remove background noise from calls, but it now offers meeting recording and transcription. Audio is recorded in tracks, so you can listen to a single speaker if you want, or see at a glance who talked and when. It was also the best of all the meeting-centric services at identifying action items. But overall, it includes fewer tools than other services, and its audio features require more setup steps.

[Zoom](https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/out/link/6626/19305/4/229212/?merchant=Zoom) offers built-in closed captioning, transcripts, AI meeting summaries, and chat to quickly catch you up on what has already been said in the meeting. Each feature works well individually, but they’re scattered throughout Zoom’s app and settings, making it more difficult to refer to notes from previous meetings than in dedicated meeting-recording tools. Zoom’s AI features also require a paid plan, which limits single users’ ability to get the full benefit.
